clusters.title: 'Scary' tropical cyclone leaves tourist town 'trashed' and cut off
clusters.neutral_headline: Cyclone Narelle Devastates Australian Tourist Town

items.title: 'Scary' tropical cyclone leaves tourist town 'trashed' and cut off
items.standfirst: The tourist town of Exmouth in Western Australia is completely cut off tonight as the clean-up from ex-tropical cyclone Narelle begins .
items.content: The tourist town of Exmouth in Western Australia is completely cut off tonight as the clean-up from ex-tropical cyclone Narelle begins .Narelle weakened to a subtropical low as it moved south-east, inland from Geraldton.It is expected to leave the mainland between 8pm and 9pm (11pm - 12am AEDT) through the Great Australian Bight between Albany and Esperance.READ MORE: The rise of Looksmaxxing: 'Creating shame and secrecy among young men' The system brought record rainfall to Exmouth last night, dumping 350mm rain and battering the town with 250km/h wind gusts. "It's trashed, the joint is trashed," resident Allan Allder told 9News.With roads flooded and the airport damaged, the Pilbara town is tonight cut off from every angle."There is no current plan to get in and out," Exmouth Shire President Matthew Nikkula told 9News."Unfortunately air travel is ceased because of the damage to the runways and the terminals and we have no way of landing a plane or unloading or reloading it."In preparing for the weather event, locals in Exmouth hit the shops to stockpile on supplies, which are now in short supply while the town reamins cut off.Further south, Carnarvon banana growers were spared the direct hit first forecast but were still left devastated, after the powerful storm destroyed hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of crops.Banana grower Les Ball said he has lost upwards of 80 per cent of his crop in the regions third natural disaster so far this year. "It's not nice that's for sure, but its just the territory and the industry that we're in," Ball told 9News.It marks the third natural disaster this year for growers, who have already contended with a heatwave, as well as Cyclone Mitchell, which decimated crops last month.Despite the devastation, growers say it won't impact banana prices at the supermarket.Only about 15 percent of our bananas come from WA, the majority are from Queensland."It will be a while until you see a WA banana back on shelves but that's OK there's plenty in Queensland," Ball said.Narelle was downgraded to a category 2 system early this morning, after making landfall through Carnarvon's south-east.It is the second longest-living system behind Cyclone Nathan, according to the Bureau of Meteorology.Perth's wettest March day in 92 yearsResidents in Mandurah copped some of the worst rainfall totals, with more than 100mm coming down in 24 hours."[This] is well above the March average of only 15mm and a new record for the station," Jessica Lingard from the BoM said.It was also wet on the coast, with Swanbourne getting 78mm, while the city lashed with close to 63mm, making it Perth's wettest March day since 1934.The SES received more than 120 calls for help across the metro area since midnight."Quite a low number really, given the weather that we've had," Department of Fire and Emergency Services Commissioner Darren Klemm said.'By far the worst, the most scary'The cyclone made landfall yesterday for the third time, crossing south of Coral Bay around 3.15pm (6.15pm AEDT) as a category 3 storm, before whipping up winds of up to 250km/h at Exmouth.The holiday hotspot was drenched in a year's worth of rain in just 12 hours. The historic system - which had already hit Queensland and the Northern Territory before sweeping out to sea and re-intensifying - was expected to impact an 800km stretch of coastline from the Pilbara down through the Gascoyne region yesterday.READ MORE: Queensland could fast-track the opening of an oil lake the size of Singapore to ease pain of fuel crisis The crossing of the "very destructive core" came after the severe tropical cyclone pummelled parts of the West Australian coastline, ripping roofs from homes in Exmouth as it tracked south.One resident , who has lived through a number of cyclones in Exmouth, told 9News this was "by far the worst, the most scary" storm she's experienced.The weather system whipped up dust storms which painted Carnarvon and Shark Bay hues from bright orange to red."You could taste the dust, it was in your mouth, in your eyes and everything," Kerrie Shepherd from Shark Bay Caravan Park told 9News. "It was apocalyptic, it was bizarre, it was something I don't think many people will see it in their lives either. West Australian Premier Roger Cook said it was a confronting situation for residents and emergency services. "We know everyone in the north-west is made of tough stuff, but our thoughts are with you all," Cook said.Dozens of emergency services personnel were sent to the region before the storm hit to be in place for the clean up.READ MORE: Easter road trips canned, cafes weigh surcharge as fuel prices stingWhile holiday operators closed on Friday and and cancelled bookings just a week out from the Easter holidays."That's just the ride that we're on, and the place that we live in up here," Dave Gillespie from Exmouth Escape Resort told 9News."It's one of those extra burdens that we've got to put up with."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Baby killed, woman and child seriously injured in rideshare crash
clusters.neutral_headline: Baby killed in rideshare crash in Perth

items.title: Baby killed, woman and child seriously injured in rideshare crash
items.standfirst: P olice are investigating the tragic single-vehicle crash that killed a baby and seriously injured two others in Perth's south-east.
items.content: P olice are investigating a tragic single-vehicle crash that killed a baby and left two others seriously injured in Perth's south-east overnight.A silver Toyota Kluger was travelling along Nicholson Road in Lynwood around 3.40am when it veered off the wet road and into a bus stop before hitting a tree, police said.Three passengers - a 32-year-old woman, a seven-month-old baby and a three-year-old child - were all seriously injured in the crash.READ MORE: 'Businesses are going under now': Truckies knocks Albo's fuel crisis plan Neighbours have told 9News several people rushed to help the woman who got out of the car with the baby, witnesses giving the infant CPR. The woman and the seven-month-old were rushed to Fiona Stanley Hospital, where the baby sadly died.The three-year-old was taken to Perth Children's Hospital with serious injuries.A 35-year-old man, who is a rideshare driver, sustained minor injuries.Police are now investigating if the children were secured in booster seats, which is not a legal requirement for taxis and rideshare vehicles in WA.Anyone who may have witnessed the crash, or saw the silver Kluger travelling in the area beforehand, is urged to contact police. Anyone with dashcam or mobile phone vision relating to this crash can upload it directly to investigators via this link.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here
